title: Calculating Parameter Budgets
concept: Parameter distribution is a key architectural choice, balancing memory footprints against model capacity. Boss explain ends with: 'Next up: Scaling Laws: Compute vs. Data.'

exercises.prompt: What does a 'parameter budget' refer to in machine learning architecture?

options.text: A limit on the total number of weights in a model
The financial cost of renting cloud GPUs
The number of training documents in a dataset

exercises.explain: A parameter budget is a design constraint that limits the total size of a neural network so it can run efficiently on target hardware.

cards.title: Building within Limits
cards.body: Imagine building a massive virtual city, but you only have a single building block kit. If you spend all your blocks building one giant skyscraper, you won't have enough blocks left for roads, houses, or parks! In AI, we face the same challenge: we have to budget our parameters carefully.

cards.title: What is a Parameter Budget?
cards.body: A parameter budget is the maximum number of weights (parameters) a model can have to fit within the memory limits of your hardware. Calculating this budget helps us decide how large each part of our neural network can be before we run out of memory.

cards.title: The Suitcase Analogy
cards.body: Think of parameter budgets like packing a suitcase for a flight. You have a strict weight limit (your hardware's RAM). You must decide how much space to give to different items (attention layers, feed-forward networks, embeddings) so you don't exceed the limit while still bringing everything you need to survive the trip.

cards.title: A Budget in Action
cards.body: If you are building a small language model to run on a smartphone, you might have a strict budget of 1.5 billion parameters. To stay under this, you might allocate 1.2 billion parameters to the main transformer blocks, 200 million to word embeddings, and 100 million to the final classification layer.

cards.title: Myth: More is Always Better Everywhere
cards.body: Many think that to make a model better, you should just add parameters everywhere equally. In reality, some layers benefit much more from extra parameters than others. Smart AI designers spend their budget where it impacts performance the most, like deep attention layers.

cards.title: Allocating the Budget
cards.body: Different parts of a model consume different amounts of your parameter budget. We must balance them carefully.

left.text: Takes up 70-80% of the parameter budget. This is where the model does its heavy reasoning.

right.text: Takes up 10-20% of the budget. This translates words into numbers.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following is the main constraint that defines a model's parameter budget?

options.text: The speed of the internet connection
The memory limits of the hardware (RAM/VRAM)
The size of the training dataset
The programming language used to write the model

exercises.explain: Hardware memory (RAM) is the physical constraint that dictates how many parameters we can load and compute at once.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Increasing the parameter budget of a model generally increases its hardware memory footprint during training and inference.
exercises.explain: A model with more parameters requires more memory to store and calculate, which increases the hardware requirements.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete the rule of parameter budgeting.
exercises.sentence: A parameter budget limits the total number of learned _____ we can use in our model.
exercises.explain: Every weight and bias in a model is a parameter, and their sum must stay within the memory limits of the deployment hardware.

exercises.prompt: Identify the incorrect advice regarding parameter distribution in a transformer model.

options.text: AI Model A: 'I recommend allocating 90% of your parameter budget to the token embedding table and 10% to the actual transformer layers.'
AI Model B: 'I recommend allocating the bulk of your parameter budget to the core transformer layers, leaving a smaller portion for embeddings.'
Human Engineer: 'We should balance the budget so the core layers have enough capacity to model complex relationships, while keeping embeddings compact.'

exercises.explain: AI Model A is the fake assistant because modern LLMs typically allocate the vast majority of their parameters to the core attention/feed-forward layers, not the input embedding layer.

exercises.prompt: You have a strict total hardware parameter budget of 2 billion parameters. If you allocate 1.5 billion parameters to the core transformer layers, how much of your budget remains for the embedding and prediction heads?

options.text: 25% of the total budget (500 million parameters)
10% of the total budget (200 million parameters)
50% of the total budget (1 billion parameters)
75% of the total budget (1.5 billion parameters)

exercises.explain: Superb calculation! You have mastered the art of balancing parameter allocation to fit hardware limits while maximizing capacity. Next up: Scaling Laws: Compute vs. Data.
